exercises.title: All Five Data Types
exercises.description: Demonstrate all five Python data types (int, float, str, bool, None) and their type conversions in a single program.
exercises.starterCode:
# TODO: Create one variable of each type
integer_val = 42
float_val = 3.14
string_val = "Python"
bool_val = True
none_val = None

# TODO: Print each value with its type
print(f"{integer_val} -> {type(integer_val)}")
print(f"{float_val} -> {type(float_val)}")
print(f"{string_val} -> {type(string_val)}")
print(f"{bool_val} -> {type(bool_val)}")
print(f"{none_val} -> {type(none_val)}")

# TODO: Demonstrate conversions
print(int(float_val))      # 3
print(float(integer_val))  # 42.0
print(str(bool_val))       # 'True'
print(bool(integer_val))   # True
hints: Each type has its own conversion function: int(), float(), str(), bool().

exercises.title: Contact Card Formatter
exercises.description: Build a contact card using string methods, f-string formatting, and type conversions. Clean and format the raw data.
exercises.starterCode:
# Raw contact data (messy)
raw_name = "  alice JOHNSON  "
raw_email = "  Alice.Johnson@EMAIL.COM  "
raw_phone = "555-867-5309"
age_str = "28"

# TODO: Clean and format the data
full_name = raw_name.strip().title()
email = raw_email.strip().lower()
phone = raw_phone
age = int(age_str)

# TODO: Generate a username: first initial + last name, lowercase, no spaces
name_parts = full_name.split()
username = (name_parts[0][0] + name_parts[1]).lower()

# TODO: Print the contact card
print("=" * 35)
print(f"Name:     {full_name}")
print(f"Email:    {email}")
print(f"Phone:    {phone}")
print(f"Age:      {age}")
print(f"Username: {username}")
print("=" * 35)
exercises.expectedOutput:
===================================
Name:     Alice Johnson
Email:    alice.johnson@email.com
Phone:    555-867-5309
Age:      28
Username: ajohnson
===================================
hints: strip() removes whitespace; title() capitalizes each word; lower() converts to lowercase.
split() breaks the name into parts; [0][0] gets the first letter.

exercises.title: Data Type Mini Quiz
exercises.description: Test your understanding of all Course 2 concepts. Each print statement should produce a specific output — complete the code.
exercises.starterCode:
# Booleans from comparisons
print(10 > 5)           # True
print(10 == "10")       # False (different types)
print(bool(""))         # False
print(bool("False"))    # True (non-empty string!)

# None checks
result = None
print(result is None)   # True
print(result is not None)  # False

# String methods
text = "  Python 3.12  "
print(text.strip())
print(text.strip().startswith("Python"))
print(len(text.strip()))

# Type conversion chain
print(str(int(float("3.9"))))
exercises.expectedOutput:
True
False
False
True
True
False
Python 3.12
True
10
3
hints: 10 == '10' is False — Python does not coerce types in comparisons.
bool('False') is True because 'False' is a non-empty string.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Course 2 Essentials
warmup.description: Quick review of all Course 2 core topics.
warmup.starterCode:
# Types
print(type(42))          # int
print(type(3.14))        # float
print(type("hi"))        # str
print(type(True))        # bool
print(type(None))        # NoneType

# Constants style
MAX_RETRIES = 3
current_retry = 0
print(f"Retry {current_retry} of {MAX_RETRIES}")
